clean_output:
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
summary: Experienced developers used AI coding agents as supervised, task-bounded productivity tools rather than autonomous replacements.
review_confidence: high
mechanisms:
  - claim_text: Experienced developers retained control over software design and implementation by planning work, supervising agent behavior, and validating outputs against core software quality standards.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-05
      - evidence-06
    mechanism_text: The source attributes retained human control to an expert-mediated workflow in which developers plan work in advance, supervise agent behavior during execution, validate outputs, and enforce fundamental software quality attributes.
    bounds: This is an operational mechanism drawn from reported practice, not a deeper causal account of which control step produces the observed benefit or where supervision cost may outweigh gains.
    confidence: high
bounded_claims:
  - claim_text: Experienced developers in this study viewed AI agents as a productivity boost rather than as autonomous substitutes for their own work.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-01
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-05
    bounds: The evidence supports reported productivity benefit and retained human agency in this study context, but it does not quantify the size of the benefit or justify generalization beyond experienced developers under similar supervision conditions.
  - claim_text: Developers in this study treated AI agents as suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks and unsuitable for complex tasks.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-07
      - evidence-08
    bounds: The evidence supports a task-bound limit on usefulness, not a causal explanation of why complex tasks fail; the claim should remain restricted to observed suitability boundaries.
  - claim_text: Positive developer sentiment toward AI agents in this study was conditional on the developers' ability to compensate for agent limitations, not on trust in agent autonomy alone.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-12
article_id: art-2026-03-04-021
summary: Experienced developers used AI coding agents as supervised, task-bounded productivity tools rather than autonomous replacements.
review_confidence: high
mechanisms:
  - claim_text: Experienced developers retained control over software design and implementation by planning work, supervising agent behavior, and validating outputs against core software quality standards.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-03
      - evidence-04
      - evidence-05
      - evidence-06
    mechanism_text: The source attributes retained human control to an expert-mediated workflow in which developers plan work in advance, supervise agent behavior during execution, validate outputs, and enforce fundamental software quality attributes.
    bounds: This is an operational mechanism drawn from reported practice, not a deeper causal account of which control step produces the observed benefit or where supervision cost may outweigh gains.
    confidence: high
bounded_claims:
  - claim_text: Experienced developers in this study viewed AI agents as a productivity boost rather than as autonomous substitutes for their own work.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-01
      - evidence-02
      - evidence-05
    bounds: The evidence supports reported productivity benefit and retained human agency in this study context, but it does not quantify the size of the benefit or justify generalization beyond experienced developers under similar supervision conditions.
  - claim_text: Developers in this study treated AI agents as suitable for well-described, straightforward tasks and unsuitable for complex tasks.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-07
      - evidence-08
    bounds: The evidence supports a task-bound limit on usefulness, not a causal explanation of why complex tasks fail; the claim should remain restricted to observed suitability boundaries.
  - claim_text: Positive developer sentiment toward AI agents in this study was conditional on the developers' ability to compensate for agent limitations, not on trust in agent autonomy alone.
    evidence_ids:
      - evidence-09
      - evidence-12
    bounds: The evidence shows conditional positivity tied to developers' belief that their own expertise can offset agent weaknesses, while cited prior studies show weaker outcomes elsewhere; this supports a context-sensitive bounded claim, not a general mechanism of agent success.
